Authors: Yue Sun; Yeqiang Qian; Zhe Wang; Tianhui Li; Chunxiang Wang; Ming Yang

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.799 (must_read)

Reliable perception is essential for autonomous driving systems to operate safely under diverse real-world traffic conditions. However, camera- and LiDAR-based perception systems suffer from performance degradation under adverse weather and lighting conditions, limiting their robustness and large-scale deployment in intelligent transportation systems. Radar-vision fusion provides a promising alternative by combining the environmental robustness and cost efficiency of millimeter-wave (mmWave) radar with the rich semantic information captured by cameras. Nevertheless, conventional 3D radar measurements lack height resolution and remain highly sparse, while emerging 4D mmWave radar introduces elevation information but also brings challenges such as signal noise and large data volume. To address these issues, this paper proposes RadarXFormer, a 3D object detection framework that enables efficient cross-modal fusion between 4D radar spectra and RGB images. Instead of relying on sparse radar point clouds, RadarXFormer directly leverages raw radar spectra and constructs an efficient 3D representation that reduces data volume while preserving complete 3D spatial information. The "X" highlights the proposed cross-dimension (3D-2D) fusion mechanism, in which multi-scale 3D spherical radar feature cubes are fused with complementary 2D image feature maps. Experiments on the K-Radar dataset demonstrate improved detection accuracy and robustness under challenging conditions while maintaining real-time inference capability.

item.title: A Joint Masked Spectral Group Framework for Hyperspectral Representation Learning
item.dc:creator: Minzhen Cao
Hao Deng
Bowen Du
Shengjie Zhao
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Given the scarcity of labeled data, hyperspectral image (HSI) analysis has increasingly relied on self-supervised representation learning. Masked reconstruction and contrastive learning are two dominant paradigms that emphasize spectral–spatial fidelity and feature discrimination, respectively, yet their objectives are not naturally aligned in HSI: reconstruction is vulnerable to redundancy-induced shortcut solutions, while contrastive learning may under-emphasize subtle but class-critical spectral cues. Motivated by this gap, we propose a joint Masked Spectral Group (MSG) framework for hyperspectral representation learning that couples the two objectives through a shared spectral-group representation and objective-consistent structured masking. At its core, MSG builds on Spectral Group Embedding and Masking (SGEM), which organizes contiguous bands into compact groups and performs group-wise masking to suppress redundancy while preserving coherent spectral context for stable view formation. This design encourages reconstruction and discrimination to complement rather than interfere with each other, yielding representations that are both discriminative and transferable. Extensive experiments under linear evaluation and few-shot fine-tuning across multiple downstream benchmarks demonstrate that MSG consistently outperforms strong masked-reconstruction and contrastive learning baselines, achieving higher accuracy, more stable class-wise performance, and stronger transferability. Published: 2026-03-17T20:24:07+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.799 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Minzhen Cao; Hao Deng; Bowen Du; Shengjie Zhao

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/tgrs.2026.3674733

Relevance: 0.799 (must_read)

Given the scarcity of labeled data, hyperspectral image (HSI) analysis has increasingly relied on self-supervised representation learning. Masked reconstruction and contrastive learning are two dominant paradigms that emphasize spectral–spatial fidelity and feature discrimination, respectively, yet their objectives are not naturally aligned in HSI: reconstruction is vulnerable to redundancy-induced shortcut solutions, while contrastive learning may under-emphasize subtle but class-critical spectral cues. Motivated by this gap, we propose a joint Masked Spectral Group (MSG) framework for hyperspectral representation learning that couples the two objectives through a shared spectral-group representation and objective-consistent structured masking. At its core, MSG builds on Spectral Group Embedding and Masking (SGEM), which organizes contiguous bands into compact groups and performs group-wise masking to suppress redundancy while preserving coherent spectral context for stable view formation. This design encourages reconstruction and discrimination to complement rather than interfere with each other, yielding representations that are both discriminative and transferable. Extensive experiments under linear evaluation and few-shot fine-tuning across multiple downstream benchmarks demonstrate that MSG consistently outperforms strong masked-reconstruction and contrastive learning baselines, achieving higher accuracy, more stable class-wise performance, and stronger transferability.

item.title: Improved and Accelerated Text-to-Image Generation with Collect, Reflect, and Refine
item.dc:creator: Shitong Shao
Zikai Zhou
Dian Xie
Yuetong Fang
Tian Ye
Lichen Bai
Bo Han
Zeke Xie
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
item.description: Recently, enhancing the generative capability of text-to-image (T2I) models has become a promising direction in both academia and industry. Prior studies often focused on either improving generative quality or reducing inference latency, but typically failed to improve both quality and speed simultaneously. Moreover, existing inference-enhancement methods do not achieve significant improvements simultaneously across both diffusion models (DMs) and autoregressive models (ARMs). In this paper, we introduce a general tuning-based inference-enhancement framework, named C o R e C o R e 2 {\bm {CoRe}^{2}} , which is the first to simultaneously achieve significant generative quality and reduced inference overhead across DMs and ARMs, to the best of our knowledge. C o R e C o R e 2 {\bm {CoRe}^{2}} comprises three stages: C o C o l l e c t {\bm {Co}llect} , R e R e f l e c t {\bm {Re}flect} , and R e R e f i n e {\bm {Re}fine} . During the Collect stage, classifier-free guidance (CFG) trajectories are collected and subsequently used in the Reflect stage to train a weak model capable of reflecting the “easy-to-learn” content. Finally, during the Refine stage, CoRe 2 ^{2} can utilize the trained weak model to achieve speedup and performance gain in inference. Specifically, in the early sampling steps, CoRe 2 ^{2} employs weak-to-strong guidance to refine the “difficult-to-learn” and realistic content, thereby improving generative quality. In the later sampling steps, CoRe 2 ^{2} can use the weak model to generate “easy-to-learn” content instead of CFG, dramatically reducing inference time. Experimental outcomes substantiates CoRe 2 ^{2} achieve significant performance improvements on HPD v2, Pick-of-Pic, Drawbench, GenEval, and T2I-Compbench across SDXL, SD3.5, FLUX and LlamaGen. Notably, for SD3.5, CoRe 2 ^{2} can be seamlessly integrated with the state-of-the-art inference-enhancement algorithm Z-Sampling, outperforming it with winning rates of 73% and 69% on PickScore and AES even with less time. Published: 2026-03-17T20:23:57+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence Score: 0.799 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Shitong Shao; Zikai Zhou; Dian Xie; Yuetong Fang; Tian Ye; Lichen Bai; Bo Han; Zeke Xie

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence

DOI: 10.1109/tpami.2026.3674984

Relevance: 0.799 (must_read)

Recently, enhancing the generative capability of text-to-image (T2I) models has become a promising direction in both academia and industry. Prior studies often focused on either improving generative quality or reducing inference latency, but typically failed to improve both quality and speed simultaneously. Moreover, existing inference-enhancement methods do not achieve significant improvements simultaneously across both diffusion models (DMs) and autoregressive models (ARMs). In this paper, we introduce a general tuning-based inference-enhancement framework, named C o R e C o R e 2 {\bm {CoRe}^{2}} , which is the first to simultaneously achieve significant generative quality and reduced inference overhead across DMs and ARMs, to the best of our knowledge. C o R e C o R e 2 {\bm {CoRe}^{2}} comprises three stages: C o C o l l e c t {\bm {Co}llect} , R e R e f l e c t {\bm {Re}flect} , and R e R e f i n e {\bm {Re}fine} . During the Collect stage, classifier-free guidance (CFG) trajectories are collected and subsequently used in the Reflect stage to train a weak model capable of reflecting the “easy-to-learn” content. Finally, during the Refine stage, CoRe 2 ^{2} can utilize the trained weak model to achieve speedup and performance gain in inference. Specifically, in the early sampling steps, CoRe 2 ^{2} employs weak-to-strong guidance to refine the “difficult-to-learn” and realistic content, thereby improving generative quality. In the later sampling steps, CoRe 2 ^{2} can use the weak model to generate “easy-to-learn” content instead of CFG, dramatically reducing inference time. Experimental outcomes substantiates CoRe 2 ^{2} achieve significant performance improvements on HPD v2, Pick-of-Pic, Drawbench, GenEval, and T2I-Compbench across SDXL, SD3.5, FLUX and LlamaGen. Notably, for SD3.5, CoRe 2 ^{2} can be seamlessly integrated with the state-of-the-art inference-enhancement algorithm Z-Sampling, outperforming it with winning rates of 73% and 69% on PickScore and AES even with less time.

item.title: Phenotype-aware Imitation Learning for Small, Oriented Object Detection in Remote Sensing
item.dc:creator: Tong Niu
Qizhi Teng
Xiaohai He
Honggang Chen
Linbo Qing
Shengfeng He
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Imitation learning has emerged as a promising paradigm for detecting small, arbitrarily oriented objects in remote sensing imagery. However, existing methods largely focus on global feature alignment between object instances and high-quality exemplars. This often leads to over-homogenized features across instances and distortion of intrinsic semantic structures, which in turn introduces geometric ambiguities in orientation, shape, and scale. Such issues are especially detrimental in remote sensing, where small objects exhibit significant variation and complex spatial distributions. To address these limitations, we propose a phenotype-aware imitation learning (PIL) framework that explicitly preserves geometric diversity and semantic structure for accurate object localization. PIL consists of two components: a geometric disentangling branch and a semantic structure registration branch. The geometric disentangling branch learns instance-specific deformation fields that adaptively normalize orientation, scale, and shape variations between exemplars and instances. By disentangling geometric differences, this branch allows the imitation process to focus on meaningful semantic discrepancies. The semantic structure registration branch further improves alignment by decoupling imitation into registration and imitation spaces. In the registration space, instances align their semantic structures by modeling spatial relations within the exemplar’s global context. In the imitation space, features that have been geometrically normalized and semantically aligned perform pixel-wise imitation, supervised by a mixed-grained contrastive loss (MC-loss) that balances both local and global semantic cues. To mitigate geometric detail loss in cross-scale imitation , we also introduce a contextual micro-to-macro feature pyramid network (CM2-FPN). This module levarages low-layer spatial detail to complement the contextual information of large objects in high-layer features. Extensive experime... Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

Relevance: 0.798 (must_read)

Imitation learning has emerged as a promising paradigm for detecting small, arbitrarily oriented objects in remote sensing imagery. However, existing methods largely focus on global feature alignment between object instances and high-quality exemplars. This often leads to over-homogenized features across instances and distortion of intrinsic semantic structures, which in turn introduces geometric ambiguities in orientation, shape, and scale. Such issues are especially detrimental in remote sensing, where small objects exhibit significant variation and complex spatial distributions. To address these limitations, we propose a phenotype-aware imitation learning (PIL) framework that explicitly preserves geometric diversity and semantic structure for accurate object localization. PIL consists of two components: a geometric disentangling branch and a semantic structure registration branch. The geometric disentangling branch learns instance-specific deformation fields that adaptively normalize orientation, scale, and shape variations between exemplars and instances. By disentangling geometric differences, this branch allows the imitation process to focus on meaningful semantic discrepancies. The semantic structure registration branch further improves alignment by decoupling imitation into registration and imitation spaces. In the registration space, instances align their semantic structures by modeling spatial relations within the exemplar’s global context. In the imitation space, features that have been geometrically normalized and semantically aligned perform pixel-wise imitation, supervised by a mixed-grained contrastive loss (MC-loss) that balances both local and global semantic cues. To mitigate geometric detail loss in cross-scale imitation , we also introduce a contextual micro-to-macro feature pyramid network (CM2-FPN). This module levarages low-layer spatial detail to complement the contextual information of large objects in high-layer features. Extensive experime...

item.title: Occlusion-Resistant Object Detection for Dual-View Airborne Platforms
item.dc:creator: Haining Zhao
Zhiguang Shi
Yu Zhang
Yongchang Li
Qian Wang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Infrared object detection in airborne surveillance faces significant challenges, particularly when targets are occluded and viewpoints are sparse and dynamic. Existing multi-view methods typically rely on dense camera arrays or precise static calibration, rendering them ill-suited for ad-hoc dual-UAV deployments characterized by asynchronous capture and sensor noise. To bridge this gap, we propose a Dual-Platform Collaborative Occlusion-Resistant Framework tailored for resource-constrained airborne platforms. This system-level architecture integrates three synergistic components to address these constraints: (1) a Hybrid Geometric-Semantic Registration (HGSR) scheme that establishes a robust spatial foundation by combining coarse physical projection with fine-grained semantic correction to align asynchronous views; (2) a Multi-Scale Enhanced Dual-Domain Strip Attention Module (MS-DSAM) that extracts occlusion-invariant features by jointly modeling spatial strip patterns and frequency-domain spectral consistency; and (3) a Dual-View Localization Loss (DLL), formulated as a weakly supervised regularization mechanism, which enforces cross-view semantic and geometric consistency without requiring dense supervision. Extensive experiments on the self-constructed DualView-Occlusion dataset and standard benchmarks (Wild-track, MultiviewX) demonstrate that our method achieves 58.9% mAP50 on small targets under moderate occlusion (Dataset A) using only two mobile views, outperforming state-of-the-art methods that rely on three or more fixed views. The system operates at 23.31 FPS, meeting real-time requirements for UAV edge deployment. Published: 2026-03-17T20:24:07+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.797 (must_read)

Relevance: 0.797 (must_read)

Infrared object detection in airborne surveillance faces significant challenges, particularly when targets are occluded and viewpoints are sparse and dynamic. Existing multi-view methods typically rely on dense camera arrays or precise static calibration, rendering them ill-suited for ad-hoc dual-UAV deployments characterized by asynchronous capture and sensor noise. To bridge this gap, we propose a Dual-Platform Collaborative Occlusion-Resistant Framework tailored for resource-constrained airborne platforms. This system-level architecture integrates three synergistic components to address these constraints: (1) a Hybrid Geometric-Semantic Registration (HGSR) scheme that establishes a robust spatial foundation by combining coarse physical projection with fine-grained semantic correction to align asynchronous views; (2) a Multi-Scale Enhanced Dual-Domain Strip Attention Module (MS-DSAM) that extracts occlusion-invariant features by jointly modeling spatial strip patterns and frequency-domain spectral consistency; and (3) a Dual-View Localization Loss (DLL), formulated as a weakly supervised regularization mechanism, which enforces cross-view semantic and geometric consistency without requiring dense supervision. Extensive experiments on the self-constructed DualView-Occlusion dataset and standard benchmarks (Wild-track, MultiviewX) demonstrate that our method achieves 58.9% mAP50 on small targets under moderate occlusion (Dataset A) using only two mobile views, outperforming state-of-the-art methods that rely on three or more fixed views. The system operates at 23.31 FPS, meeting real-time requirements for UAV edge deployment.

item.title: Concept Drift and Long-Tailed Distribution in Fine-Grained Visual Categorization: Benchmark and Method
item.dc:creator: Shuo Ye
Shiming Chen
Ruxin Wang
Tianxu Wu
Salman Khan
Fahad Shahbaz Khan
Ling Shao
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
item.description: Data is the foundation for the development of computer vision, and the establishment of datasets plays an important role in advancing the techniques of fine-grained visual categorization (FGVC). In the existing FGVC datasets used in computer vision, it is generally assumed that each collected instance has fixed characteristics and the distribution of different categories is relatively balanced. In contrast, the real world scenario reveals the fact that the characteristics of instances tend to vary with time and exhibit a long-tailed distribution. Hence, the collected datasets may mislead the optimization of the fine-grained classifiers, resulting in unpleasant performance in real applications. Starting from the real-world conditions and to promote the practical progress of fine-grained visual categorization, we present a Concept Drift and Long-Tailed Distribution (CDLT) dataset. Specifically, the dataset is collected by gathering 11195 images of 250 instances in different species for 47 consecutive months in their natural contexts. The collection process involves dozens of crowd workers for photographing and domain experts for labeling. Meanwhile, we propose a feature recombination framework to address the learning challenges associated with CDLT. Experimental results validate the efficacy of our method while also highlighting the limitations of popular large vision-language models (e.g., CLIP) in the context of long-tailed distributions. This emphasizes the significance of CDLT as a benchmark for investigating these challenges. The dataset is available at: https://github.com/SYe-hub/CDLT Published: 2026-03-17T20:23:57+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence Score: 0.795 (must_read)

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence

DOI: 10.1109/tpami.2026.3674763

Relevance: 0.795 (must_read)

Data is the foundation for the development of computer vision, and the establishment of datasets plays an important role in advancing the techniques of fine-grained visual categorization (FGVC). In the existing FGVC datasets used in computer vision, it is generally assumed that each collected instance has fixed characteristics and the distribution of different categories is relatively balanced. In contrast, the real world scenario reveals the fact that the characteristics of instances tend to vary with time and exhibit a long-tailed distribution. Hence, the collected datasets may mislead the optimization of the fine-grained classifiers, resulting in unpleasant performance in real applications. Starting from the real-world conditions and to promote the practical progress of fine-grained visual categorization, we present a Concept Drift and Long-Tailed Distribution (CDLT) dataset. Specifically, the dataset is collected by gathering 11195 images of 250 instances in different species for 47 consecutive months in their natural contexts. The collection process involves dozens of crowd workers for photographing and domain experts for labeling. Meanwhile, we propose a feature recombination framework to address the learning challenges associated with CDLT. Experimental results validate the efficacy of our method while also highlighting the limitations of popular large vision-language models (e.g., CLIP) in the context of long-tailed distributions. This emphasizes the significance of CDLT as a benchmark for investigating these challenges. The dataset is available at: https://github.com/SYe-hub/CDLT

item.title: PKINet-v2: Towards Powerful and Efficient Poly-Kernel Remote Sensing Object Detection
item.dc:creator: Xinhao Cai
Liulei Li
Gensheng Pei
Zeren Sun
Yazhou Yao
Wenguan Wang
item.prism:publicationName: arXiv
item.description: Object detection in remote sensing images (RSIs) is challenged by the coexistence of geometric and spatial complexity: targets may appear with diverse aspect ratios, while spanning a wide range of object sizes under varied contexts. Existing RSI backbones address the two challenges separately, either by adopting anisotropic strip kernels to model slender targets or by using isotropic large kernels to capture broader context. However, such isolated treatments lead to complementary drawbacks: the strip-only design can disrupt spatial coherence for regular-shaped objects and weaken tiny details, whereas isotropic large kernels often introduce severe background noise and geometric mismatch for slender structures. In this paper, we extend PKINet, and present a powerful and efficient backbone that jointly handles both challenges within a unified paradigm named Poly Kernel Inception Network v2 (PKINet-v2). PKINet-v2 synergizes anisotropic axial-strip convolutions with isotropic square kernels and builds a multi-scope receptive field, preserving fine-grained local textures while progressively aggregating long-range context across scales. To enable efficient deployment, we further introduce a Heterogeneous Kernel Re-parameterization (HKR) Strategy that fuses all heterogeneous branches into a single depth-wise convolution for inference, eliminating fragmented kernel launches without accuracy loss. Extensive experiments on four widely-used benchmarks, including DOTA-v1.0, DOTA-v1.5, HRSC2016, and DIOR-R, demonstrate that PKINet-v2 achieves state-of-the-art accuracy while delivering a $\textbf{3.9}\times$ FPS acceleration compared to PKINet-v1, surpassing previous remote sensing backbones in both effectiveness and efficiency. Published: 2026-03-17T10:17:58+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.795 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xinhao Cai; Liulei Li; Gensheng Pei; Zeren Sun; Yazhou Yao; Wenguan Wang

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.795 (must_read)

Object detection in remote sensing images (RSIs) is challenged by the coexistence of geometric and spatial complexity: targets may appear with diverse aspect ratios, while spanning a wide range of object sizes under varied contexts. Existing RSI backbones address the two challenges separately, either by adopting anisotropic strip kernels to model slender targets or by using isotropic large kernels to capture broader context. However, such isolated treatments lead to complementary drawbacks: the strip-only design can disrupt spatial coherence for regular-shaped objects and weaken tiny details, whereas isotropic large kernels often introduce severe background noise and geometric mismatch for slender structures. In this paper, we extend PKINet, and present a powerful and efficient backbone that jointly handles both challenges within a unified paradigm named Poly Kernel Inception Network v2 (PKINet-v2). PKINet-v2 synergizes anisotropic axial-strip convolutions with isotropic square kernels and builds a multi-scope receptive field, preserving fine-grained local textures while progressively aggregating long-range context across scales. To enable efficient deployment, we further introduce a Heterogeneous Kernel Re-parameterization (HKR) Strategy that fuses all heterogeneous branches into a single depth-wise convolution for inference, eliminating fragmented kernel launches without accuracy loss. Extensive experiments on four widely-used benchmarks, including DOTA-v1.0, DOTA-v1.5, HRSC2016, and DIOR-R, demonstrate that PKINet-v2 achieves state-of-the-art accuracy while delivering a $\textbf{3.9}\times$ FPS acceleration compared to PKINet-v1, surpassing previous remote sensing backbones in both effectiveness and efficiency.

item.title: C&D-CLIP: Cascaded Decoder and Deep Cross Visual Prompt Tuning for Zero-Shot Semantic Segmentation
item.dc:creator: Linchuan Li
Yongquan Liang
Zhihui Wang
Yongtang Bao
Shansong Yang
item.prism:publicationName: Pattern Recognition
item.description: In the domain of semantic segmentation, the challenge of segmenting unseen classes remains a critical research problem. However, existing approaches often overlook the intrinsic properties within these unseen categories. Zero-shot semantic segmentation aims to transfer knowledge from seen to unseen classes during inference by leveraging information from seen classes, thereby enabling the segmentation of both known and unknown categories. This paradigm has demonstrated substantial potential across various applications, including medical imaging, autonomous driving, remote sensing, agriculture, and environmental monitoring. Pre-trained vision-language models, such as CLIP, have been effectively applied to zero-shot semantic segmentation. Current CLIP-based one-stage zero-shot segmentation methods typically adopt visual prompt tuning (VPT) for fine-tuning. However, the experimental results show that VPT causes the model to rely too heavily on prompt tokens. This weakens its focus on pre-trained features, leading to overfitting of the training classes. As a result, the model’s ability to generalize to unseen classes is reduced. To address this limitation, we propose Deep Cross Visual Prompt Tuning (DCVPT), a strategy that enhances the interaction between visual features and prompt tokens by computing cross-attention, enabling more precise fine-tuning and improving adaptation to zero-shot segmentation tasks. Moreover, existing CLIP-based approaches primarily utilize visual features from the final layer for alignment with text embeddings, neglecting critical information embedded within intermediate layers, which contain rich object details. Our analysis indicates that direct aggregation of multi-level visual features enhances segmentation performance for seen classes but may degrade generalization to unseen categories due to the inherent variation in feature representations across layers, complicating alignment with textual embeddings. To address this challenge, we introduce a series of independent decoders for the intermediate and last layers. This framework generates segmentation masks by aligning multi-level visual features with text embeddings in a cascade manner, ultimately combining these masks to produce the final segmentation results. Additionally, we propose two novel components: a Feature Calibration Module and a Dual-Path Decoder, both of which enhance pixel-level generalization and improve the complementarity between visual and textual modalities. Integrating these advancements, we present an efficient zero-shot semantic segmentation model, termed C&D-CLIP. Comprehensive experiments, conducted on three public benchmarks (COCO-Stuff, PASCAL VOC 2012, and PASCAL Context), show that C&D-CLIP surpasses existing state-of-the-art methods under both “inductive” and “transductive” zero-shot settings. Published: 2026-03-18T10:06:41+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.793 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:

Journal: Pattern Recognition

However, existing approaches often overlook the intrinsic properties within these unseen categories. Zero-shot semantic segmentation aims to transfer knowledge from seen to unseen classes during inference by leveraging information from seen classes, thereby enabling the segmentation of both known and unknown categories. This paradigm has demonstrated substantial potential across various applications, including medical imaging, autonomous driving, remote sensing, agriculture, and environmental monitoring. Pre-trained vision-language models, such as CLIP, have been effectively applied to zero-shot semantic segmentation. However, the experimental results show that VPT causes the model to rely too heavily on prompt tokens. This weakens its focus on pre-trained features, leading to overfitting of the training classes. As a result, the model’s ability to generalize to unseen classes is reduced. To address this limitation, we propose Deep Cross Visual Prompt Tuning (DCVPT), a strategy that enhances the interaction between visual features and prompt tokens by computing cross-attention, enabling more precise fine-tuning and improving adaptation to zero-shot segmentation tasks. Moreover, existing CLIP-based approaches primarily utilize visual features from the final layer for alignment with text embeddings, neglecting critical information embedded within intermediate layers, which contain rich object details. Our analysis indicates that direct aggregation of multi-level visual features enhances segmentation performance for seen classes but may degrade generalization to unseen categories due to the inherent variation in feature representations across layers, complicating alignment with textual embeddings. To address this challenge, we introduce a series of independent decoders for the intermediate and last layers. This framework generates segmentation masks by aligning multi-level visual features with text embeddings in a cascade manner, ultimately combining these masks to produce the final segmentation results. Additionally, we propose two novel components: a Feature Calibration Module and a Dual-Path Decoder, both of which enhance pixel-level generalization and improve the complementarity between visual and textual modalities. Integrating these advancements, we present an efficient zero-shot semantic segmentation model, termed C&D-CLIP. Comprehensive experiments, conducted on three public benchmarks (COCO-Stuff, PASCAL VOC 2012, and PASCAL Context), show that C&D-CLIP surpasses existing state-of-the-art methods under both “inductive” and “transductive” zero-shot settings.

item.title: LLMs displaying less cognitive bias are not necessarily better decision makers
item.dc:creator: Vittoria Dentella
Marco Marelli
Luca Rinaldi
item.prism:publicationName: Nature Machine Intelligence
item.description: Large language models (LLMs) include not only social stereotypes but also cognitive biases. As researchers work to identify, characterize and rectify these biases, we encourage the scientific community to recognize that, although often seen as errors, cognitive biases can also reflect functional, context-specific adaptations in reasoning. Published: 2026-03-17T17:30:47+00:00 Venue: Nature Machine Intelligence Score: 0.793 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Vittoria Dentella; Marco Marelli; Luca Rinaldi

Journal: Nature Machine Intelligence

DOI: 10.1038/s42256-026-01208-w

Relevance: 0.793 (must_read)

Large language models (LLMs) include not only social stereotypes but also cognitive biases. As researchers work to identify, characterize and rectify these biases, we encourage the scientific community to recognize that, although often seen as errors, cognitive biases can also reflect functional, context-specific adaptations in reasoning.

item.title: Improved Gaussian Wasserstein Distance: A Smooth Adaptive New Metric for Remote Sensing Tiny Object Detection
item.dc:creator: Heng Hu
Si-Bao Chen
Jin Tang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
item.description: Due to tiny size of tiny objects, which contain only a small number of pixels and lack of appearance information, it is difficult for detectors to recognize such objects. Through experimental analysis, it is found that existing metrics are misfit and not applicable to remote sensing tiny object detection. Among them, Intersection over Union (IoU) series of metrics are extremely sensitive to slight offset of position of tiny objects. Distance metrics such as Dot Distance (DotD), Gaussian Wasserstein Distance (GWD), Normalized Wasserstein Distance (NWD) and Kullback-Leibler Divergence (KLD) either do not have scale invariance, are not symmetric, or are unable to measure probability distribution of two non-overlapping regions, which leads to their limitations when applied to remotely sensed tiny objects as well. Based on this, we propose Improved Gaussian Wasserstein Distance (IGWD). This metric not only mitigates position offset sensitivity problem, but also solves some of problems of distance metrics. IGWD has a good generalization ability and can be easily embedded into other detectors. In addition, our improved KLD metric also has good generalization ability and its performance outperforms existing metrics. Meanwhile, in order to solve problems of channel isolation of traditional convolution and computational redundancy and weight bias of attention mechanism, we propose Adaptive Channel Graph Convolution (ACGC). Based on idea of graph convolution, feature interactions between channels are effectively enhanced by fusing standard convolution with graph convolution. Ultimately, we combine the new metrics with ACGC to construct the YOLO-ACGC detector for remote sensing of tiny objects, and carry out a large number of experiments on several public datasets to verify effectiveness of method. Published: 2026-03-18T19:39:50+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia Score: 0.791 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Heng Hu; Si-Bao Chen; Jin Tang

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia

DOI: 10.1109/tmm.2026.3675527

Relevance: 0.791 (must_read)

Due to tiny size of tiny objects, which contain only a small number of pixels and lack of appearance information, it is difficult for detectors to recognize such objects. Through experimental analysis, it is found that existing metrics are misfit and not applicable to remote sensing tiny object detection. Among them, Intersection over Union (IoU) series of metrics are extremely sensitive to slight offset of position of tiny objects. Distance metrics such as Dot Distance (DotD), Gaussian Wasserstein Distance (GWD), Normalized Wasserstein Distance (NWD) and Kullback-Leibler Divergence (KLD) either do not have scale invariance, are not symmetric, or are unable to measure probability distribution of two non-overlapping regions, which leads to their limitations when applied to remotely sensed tiny objects as well. Based on this, we propose Improved Gaussian Wasserstein Distance (IGWD). This metric not only mitigates position offset sensitivity problem, but also solves some of problems of distance metrics. IGWD has a good generalization ability and can be easily embedded into other detectors. In addition, our improved KLD metric also has good generalization ability and its performance outperforms existing metrics. Meanwhile, in order to solve problems of channel isolation of traditional convolution and computational redundancy and weight bias of attention mechanism, we propose Adaptive Channel Graph Convolution (ACGC). Based on idea of graph convolution, feature interactions between channels are effectively enhanced by fusing standard convolution with graph convolution. Ultimately, we combine the new metrics with ACGC to construct the YOLO-ACGC detector for remote sensing of tiny objects, and carry out a large number of experiments on several public datasets to verify effectiveness of method.
